narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses whether applicants are likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, estimated a 52% probability that you would fail it—crossing the threshold we use for rejections to ensure strong outcomes for admitted students. As a Black male with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average, you've already shown academic capability that the model recognizes positively. However, your lower family income bracket played a supportive role in the model's view, suggesting resilience that nudged the prediction toward expecting you to pass. The decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of about 5.5, which significantly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure by signaling a dip in performance relative to peers. Your undergraduate GPA had the strongest counterbalancing effect, pulling firmly in the direction of success and highlighting it as one of your key strengths compared to most applicants. Family income came in third, with its low level working in your favor by implying determination that often correlates with passing the bar. Overall, while your full-time student status aligns with the vast majority of applicants and your LSAT score of 26 is below average, it was the combination of that low Year 3 decile overpowering your other positives that tipped the scales to a failure prediction. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted preparation—but the model prioritizes patterns from past students to minimize risks for the program. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the typical 37 range, strengthening your Year 3 performance through additional coursework or tutoring, and gathering letters emphasizing your resilience from a low-income background. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors to admission. You're clearly driven, and with focused improvements, the model—and admissions—could see you differently next time. Keep pushing; your story has real momentum.
